core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_5 because the negotiation converged on a one-item-per-agent baseline where Agent_1's early claimed anchor, Jewel, was treated as protected and high-value, while Agent_5 accepted Apple as a small concession. The outcome looks advantaged for the first position, but not from order alone: early baselines failed until private voting and reflections converged on a simple lower-trio split.
why_or_counterexample: The first position looks advantaged because Agent_1's Jewel claim became a fixed consensus anchor and final utility was far above Agent_5's Apple payoff. The counterexample is that Agent_1 did not automatically win from being first: Agent_1 rejected the consensus-like baseline in Rounds 1 and 2 when higher immediate utility was available, and consensus only arrived after Round 3 standardization.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by naming Jewel and Quill as top priorities; later public discussion repeatedly locked Jewel with Agent_1 and Quill with Agent_4 while treating Apple as a concession for Agent_5.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present. The 15 private-thinking entries converged on protecting Jewel/Quill anchors, using Stone-for-Pencil as a bridge, and resolving the lower trio with Stone to Agent_2, Pencil to Agent_3, Apple to Agent_5.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Present. Most successful-form proposals repeated the same baseline allocation; two-agent concentration proposals failed because they stripped top items or zeroed out agents.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present. Rounds 1 and 2 failed unanimity because some agents chased higher immediate payoff or vetoed losing top items; in Round 3, agents accepted baseline proposals and rejected deviations.
phase_notes.reflection: Present. The 10 reflections diagnosed the lower-trio tie-break as the blocker and recommended a simple transparent baseline while keeping Jewel and Quill fixed.

mechanisms.theme: Early Jewel anchor became the shared core
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first public stance made Jewel protection salient, and subsequent discussion and reflections treated Jewel with Agent_1 as part of the stable coalition core. That made it easier for Agent_1 to keep a very high-value item while bargaining shifted to lower items.

evidence.quote: My top priorities are Jewel and Quill
Jewel is my top priority and I intend to keep Jewel with me
Jewel with Agent_1 and Quill with Agent_4

mechanisms.theme: One-item settlement hid large value asymmetry
mechanisms.explanation: The final allocation looked balanced by item count, but Agent_1's assigned item was worth 42 to Agent_1 while Agent_5's assigned Apple was worth only 3 to Agent_5. This value asymmetry directly explains the large first-minus-last gap.

mechanisms.theme: Last position accepted a concession role
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 repeatedly framed Apple as the process lubricant rather than fighting for Jewel or Stone in the final baseline. That helped consensus form but left the last position with a low-utility item.

evidence.quote: Apple is a reasonable concession point

mechanisms.theme: Private utility maximization delayed first-position success
mechanisms.explanation: The first position did not win automatically. Agent_1 privately rejected early consensus-like proposals when another option offered more immediate utility, so the first-position edge depended on later convergence rather than simple first-mover control.

evidence.quote: lower than the best option available

mechanisms.explanation: Reflections after Round 2 identified the lower-trio split as the remaining blocker. Round 3 then repeated the same baseline that protected Agent_1's Jewel and assigned Agent_5 Apple, turning near-consensus into unanimous acceptance.

evidence.quote: Round 3 baseline allocation: Jewel to Agent_1
